definition: A false breakout that sweeps liquidity beyond a prior swing high or low, triggering stop-loss and breakout orders, then reverses and closes back inside the range. It is the manipulation phase of the Power of 3 (AMD) cycle, often occurring near a PD array (order block, fair value gap) to pair smart money orders.
identification_rules: Price breaks a prior swing high or low aggressively, then fails to sustain the breakout and closes back inside the range.
The sweep is aggressive yet shallow, barely reaching into a PD array (order block, fair value gap, consequent encroachment) and forming a wick on the higher time frame.
After the sweep, price reverses and expands in the opposite direction, confirmed by a change in state of delivery (close through opposing candles).
The manipulation leg (range extreme to sweep high/low) can be used to anchor standard deviation projections for distribution targets.
Inducement typically occurs within a consolidation range, not during a trend continuation.
On lower time frames, multiple consecutive failure swings may accumulate, forming low-resistance liquidity.
Breakout of key levels (e.g., 8:30 open, midnight open) that fails to close beyond confirms inducement.
The sweep must be accompanied by low volume or weak momentum, lacking sustained displacement.
trading_implication: Inducement signals the manipulation phase for smart money entries. After confirming higher-timeframe order flow and a PD array, traders enter upon an aggressive sweep of a recent low (bullish) or high (bearish) that closes back inside the range. Stops are placed beyond the swept level; targets are the opposite range extreme or resting liquidity. The manipulation leg can project standard deviation targets (-2 to -2.5, -4 to -4.5) for distribution. In trending markets, successive sweeps respecting the level offer repeated continuation entries.
common_mistakes: Entering on the initial sweep before a change in state of delivery confirms the move as manipulation rather than a real breakout.
Taking inducement setups without first confirming higher time frame order flow direction.
Entering when the sweep penetrates deep through equilibrium or discount/premium rather than barely tagging the PD array.
Treating an inducement sweep as a genuine breakout and trading in the direction of the lure instead of the reversal.
Looking for inducement without an associated PD array.
Entering before price closes back inside the range (below swept high or above swept low).
Confusing a manipulation sweep with a genuine breakout, leading to chasing the false move or misinterpreting a true displacement.
Failing to pair the manipulation with a point of interest (e.g., Fair Value Gap), resulting in invalid setups.
Taking entries outside of premium/discount zones (e.g., shorts in discount, longs in premium).
Ignoring the higher time frame direction (daily bias) and trading all inducement patterns blindly.
merge_notes: All three input cards are from ICT school; no school conflict. The third card uses 'Manipulation' as canonical term but is semantically identical to Inducement; merged under Inducement. Some identification rules from the third card (e.g., standard deviation projection) are preserved as they add nuance.
